patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 1/8] mm: use zone lock guard in reserve_highatomic_pageblock()
Re: [PATCH 1/8] mm: use zone lock guard in reserve_highatomic_pageblock()
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch reworks the cleanup flow for reserve_highatomic_pageblock() to eliminate a branch that depends on compiler inlining decisions, thereby simplifying the code and reducing the likelihood of regressions.

review_comments.author: Peter Zijlstra
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer questioned the need for the branch elimination, pointing out that the compiler can already inline the cleanup path due to __always_inline, and should be able to eliminate the test itself if value-tracking excludes NULL.

reviewer expressed concern that eliminating the __GUARD_IS_ERR() branch and simplifying cleanup flow would require a thorough audit of all existing users, citing that this behavior has been present since the start
review_comments.raw_body:
I'm confused, all this has __always_inline on. And the compilers should
be able to track the assignment of the variable and eliminate the test
themselves if value-tracking excludes NULL.

---

This would require very careful audit of all the current users, it's had
this behaviour from the start.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author is addressing Peter Zijlstra's feedback on eliminating the branch in free_pcppages_bulk() by removing __GUARD_IS_ERR(). The author retested the patchset and found that the NULL check is not eliminated, even when using GCC 16. They suspect that function calls between construction and destruction prevent the compiler from proving .lock is non-NULL across the scope.

Author addressed Peter Zijlstra's concern about the need for a NULL check in the destructor, explaining that it is unnecessary because the guard infrastructure prevents user code from clearing the lock after construction and before destruction.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'd expect that too, but seems it is not the case. I was looking at this
patchset rebased on top of the mm-stable build with GCC 16 with
defconfig.

    https://lore.kernel.org/all/cover.1775654118.git.d@ilvokhin.com/


$ ./scripts/bloat-o-meter /tmp/bloat-gcc-16/page_alloc.o.before /tmp/bloat-gcc-16/page_alloc.o.after | grep -v UNIQUE
add/remove: 24/24 grow/shrink: 2/1 up/down: 232/-193 (39)
Function                                     old     new   delta
free_pcppages_bulk                           577     601     +24
free_pages_exact                             153     169     +16
unreserve_highatomic_pageblock               608     607      -1
Total: Before=42366, After=42405, chg +0.09%

I looked at free_pcppages_bulk() generated code diff and got the
following differences (only relevant parts).

[...]

- mov 0x20(%rsp),%r12
- mov 0x28(%rsp),%r15
+ mov 0x20(%rsp),%r13
+ mov 0x28(%rsp),%r12
+ test %r13,%r13                       ; guard NULL branch
+ je 2ba6 <free_pcppages_bulk+0x246>

[...]

  add $0x30,%rsp
- mov %r15,%rsi
- mov %r12,%rdi
+ mov %r12,%rsi
+ mov %r13,%rdi
  pop %rbx
  pop %rbp
  pop %r12
  pop %r13
  pop %r14
  pop %r15
- jmp 2b8b <free_pcppages_bulk+0x22b>
+ jmp 2b90 <free_pcppages_bulk+0x230>
  mov $0x800,%eax
  jmp 2af3 <free_pcppages_bulk+0x193>
  mov 0x1c(%rsp),%edx
  mov %ebp,%r14d
  jmp 29ae <free_pcppages_bulk+0x4e>
- data16 cs nopw 0x0(%rax,%rax,1)
+ add $0x30,%rsp                      ; epilogue is duplicated once again.
+ pop %rbx
+ pop %rbp
+ pop %r12
+ pop %r13
+ pop %r14
+ pop %r15
+ jmp 2bb9 <free_pcppages_bulk+0x259>

So, NULL check is not eliminated and in addition compiler couldn't merge
two almost identical epilogues here. I suspect the function calls
between construction and destruction (e.g., __free_one_page()) prevent
the compiler from proving .lock is non-NULL across the scope, but this
is just speculation, I am not a compiler expert.

---

My thinking process was the following: the constructor doesn't check for
NULL either, so if something is passing NULL to begin with it should
already crash in constructor not even reaching the destructor.

The only case when we'd need the NULL check in the destructor is if
something changes .lock to NULL after construction, but before
destruction.

This can't happen:

- The guard infrastructure provides no API to clear a guard after
  construction (no_free_ptr()/retain_ptr() only work with __free, not
  guards).

- guard() creates an anonymous variable via __UNIQUE_ID, so user code                                                   
  can't reference it to modify .lock directly.

- CLASS_INIT() is the only macro that allows bypassing the constructor,                                                 
  but it is only used for fd_prepare in include/linux/file.h, not for
  any lock guards.

- Conditional (_try) variants have their own destructor via
  EXTEND_CLASS_COND() that checks __GUARD_IS_ERR() and returns early
  before reaching the base destructor. They are not affected by this
  change.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 00/28] vfs/nfsd: add support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 00/28] vfs/nfsd: add support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 01/28] filelock: pass current blocking lease to trace_break_lease_block() rather than "new_fl"
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the break_lease_block and trace_break_lease_unblock functions in the filelock code to pass the current blocking lease (fl) instead of a new lease (new_fl). This change provides more accurate information about the lease being broken. The patch also ensures that passing NULL is handled correctly for unblocking operations, as the original lease may have been freed by then.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 02/28] filelock: add support for ignoring deleg breaks for dir change events
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support to ignore delegation breaks on directory change events in NFS clients. It introduces a new flags parameter to the try_break_deleg function and modifies the __break_lease function to ignore certain types of delegation break events. This allows the client to be notified of directory changes after the fact, rather than having the server recall the delegation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 03/28] filelock: add a tracepoint to start of break_lease()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a tracepoint to the start of the break_lease() function in the Linux kernel's filelock module. The tracepoint is used to display information about the LEASE_BREAK_* flags that are being passed to the function. This allows for easier debugging and analysis of lease breaking operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 04/28] filelock: add an inode_lease_ignore_mask helper
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new helper function called `inode_lease_ignore_mask` that returns a mask of all directory change events currently ignored by any leases on an inode. The function walks the list of leases associated with the inode and ors together their ignored event flags. This information is used by NFSd to configure the fsnotify_mark mask, which determines what file system events are reported to clients. The patch introduces a new macro `IGNORE_MASK` that defines the bits for ignored directory create, delete, and rename events. The function is exported as a GPL symbol.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 05/28] fsnotify: new tracepoint in fsnotify()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint to the fsnotify function, allowing developers to see exactly how this function is being called. The tracepoint captures various parameters passed to fsnotify and provides detailed information about each call. This addition will aid in debugging and understanding the behavior of fsnotify, which is used for file system notifications.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 06/28] fsnotify: add fsnotify_modify_mark_mask()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function called fsnotify_modify_mark_mask() to the Linux kernel's fsnotify module. The function allows callers to modify an existing mark's mask by setting and clearing specific bits, and also updates the associated connector if necessary. This is required for nfsd to handle directory delegations correctly. The patch introduces a new exported function that can be used by other parts of the kernel to modify marks and their masks.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 07/28] fsnotify: add FSNOTIFY_EVENT_RENAME data type
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new data type, FSNOTIFY_EVENT_RENAME, to the fsnotify system. It allows for the notification of rename events that replace existing files, providing both the moved dentry and the overwritten target inode in the event data. This enables consumers like nfsd to atomically observe the overwritten file without needing a separate delete event. The patch updates various functions to handle this new type, including fsnotify_move(), fsnotify_data_inode(), and fsnotify_data_dentry().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 08/28] nfsd: check fl_lmops in nfsd_breaker_owns_lease()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the nfsd_breaker_owns_lease function to quickly check if a lease is an nfsd lease by verifying that its fl_lmops field matches the nfsd_lease_mng_ops operations. This optimization allows for early return in cases where the breaker does not own the lease, improving performance. The change addresses a potential inefficiency in the original code and ensures more efficient handling of leases created by nfsd.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 09/28] nfsd: add protocol support for CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for the CB_NOTIFY protocol to the nfsd kernel module. It includes updates to the xdr (External Data Representation) files, which define the format of data exchanged between the client and server in an NFSv4.x connection. The patch also removes duplicate definitions from various header files and regenerates the xdr files. Additionally, it addresses conflicts with user-space constants by removing them from the kernel API and suggesting that users obtain these values from more authoritative sources.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 10/28] nfs_common: add new NOTIFY4_* flags proposed in RFC8881bis
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds new NOTIFY4_* flags to the NFSv4 protocol as proposed in RFC8881bis. The new flags are used in GET_DIR_DELEGATION and include various notification types such as AUFLAG_VALID, CHANGE_AUTH, and CFLAG_ORDER. These additions aim to support the requirements outlined in the revised document. The patch modifies several files, including documentation, header files, and source code for NFSv4 protocol generation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 11/28] nfsd: allow nfsd to get a dir lease with an ignore mask
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the NFS server to obtain a directory lease with an ignore mask, enabling it to filter out specific notification types. The patch introduces a new function `nfsd_notify_to_ignore` that maps notification types to corresponding flags in the file lease structure. This change affects the `nfs4_alloc_init_lease` and `nfsd_get_dir_deleg` functions, which now take an additional `notify` parameter to specify the ignore mask. The patch aims to improve the flexibility of directory leases by allowing clients to customize the notifications they receive.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 12/28] nfsd: update the fsnotify mark when setting or removing a dir delegation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the fsnotify mark when setting or removing a directory delegation in the NFS server. It introduces a new helper function, nfsd_fsnotify_recalc_mask, which recalculates the mask on the nfsd_file's fsnotify_mark based on the current directory delegations on an inode. The function is called whenever a directory delegation is added or removed, ensuring that notifications are properly set up for ignored delegation events such as file creation, deletion, and renaming. This patch addresses potential issues with notification handling in the presence of directory delegations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 13/28] nfsd: make nfsd4_callback_ops->prepare operation bool return
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the nfsd4_callback_ops->prepare operation to return a boolean value indicating whether processing should continue or not. The change is made to handle allocation failures during CB_NOTIFY operations, where processing should be stopped if an allocation fails. The modification affects several callback preparation functions and ensures that the correct behavior is maintained in case of allocation errors.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 14/28] nfsd: add callback encoding and decoding linkages for CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds routines for encoding and decoding CB_NOTIFY messages in the NFS server (nfsd). It introduces new functions to handle the encoding and decoding of these messages, which are generated by the xdrgen tool. The patch also includes changes to the state.h and xdr4cb.h files to support the new functionality. The goal is to improve the handling of CB_NOTIFY messages in the NFS server.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 15/28] nfsd: use RCU to protect fi_deleg_file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a concurrency issue in the NFS server code by using RCU (Read-Copy Update) to protect access to the fi_deleg_file pointer. The problem arises when put_deleg_file() nulls out the pointer, and concurrent accesses from workqueue callbacks can dereference a NULL pointer if they race with this teardown. To fix this, the patch annotates fi_deleg_file with __rcu and converts all accessors to use RCU primitives, such as rcu_assign_pointer() for stores and rcu_dereference_protected() for reads under certain conditions. This prepares the ground for a subsequent patch that will safely acquire a reference from the CB_NOTIFY callback path without holding fi_lock.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 16/28] nfsd: add data structures for handling CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds data structures and functionality to support directory delegation CB_NOTIFY in the NFSv4 protocol. It introduces two new structs, nfsd_notify_event and nfsd4_cb_notify, which carry fsnotify events and per-delegation state for notification handling respectively. The patch also refactors alloc_init_deleg() into a common function with a pluggable sc_free callback and adds alloc_init_dir_deleg() to allocate resources needed for CB_NOTIFY encoding. Additionally, it sets up skeleton nfsd4_cb_notify_ops with done/release handlers that will be filled in when the notification path is wired up.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 17/28] nfsd: add notification handlers for dir events
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for directory events in the NFS daemon (nfsd). It introduces a new notification handler for dir events and creates a CB_NOTIFY request to handle these notifications. When a directory is created, it sets up a callback to handle the event and allocates an nfsd_notify_event object. If any delegation is affected by the event, it recalls them if anything fails along the way. The patch modifies several files in the fs/nfsd directory to implement this functionality.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 18/28] nfsd: add tracepoint to dir_event handler
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a tracepoint to the dir_event handler in the NFS server (nfsd) code, providing visibility into the fsnotify handlers. The added tracepoint is called nfsd_handle_dir_event and it captures information about directory events, including the inode number, device number, and file system mask. This allows developers to monitor and debug directory-related operations in the NFS server.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 19/28] nfsd: apply the notify mask to the delegation when requested
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the NFSv4 server to apply the requested notification mask to directory delegations when enabled by the client. If the client requests notifications without specifying NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND, no notifications are offered as the server does not provide necessary information for classic notifications. The patch ensures that the lease acquisition sets the appropriate ignore mask based on the applied notification mask.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 20/28] nfsd: add helper to marshal a fattr4 from completed args
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new helper function `nfsd4_encode_attr_vals` to the NFSv4 server code, which encodes attribute values into an XDR stream. The function takes in an XDR stream, an attribute mask, and a structure containing arguments for encoding attributes. It breaks out the encoding of attribute values from the main encoding loop, making the code more modular and easier to maintain.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 21/28] nfsd: allow nfsd4_encode_fattr4_change() to work with no export
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the nfsd4_encode_fattr4_change function to work even when there is no export available, which can occur in certain callback scenarios such as CB_NOTIFY. The change ensures that the function behaves correctly and safely in these situations by adding a check for the presence of an export before attempting to access it.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 22/28] nfsd: send basic file attributes in CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the ability to send basic file attributes in a CB_NOTIFY event, allowing clients to receive information about the inode in addition to the filename. The attributes sent include type, change time, size, and other basic stats. This is achieved by using the GETATTR operation to retrieve the necessary information from the kernel. The approach taken is to modify the nfsd4_setup_notify_entry4 function to reserve space for the attribute mask and then use the vfs_getattr function to retrieve the attributes. The retrieved attributes are then encoded into the CB_NOTIFY event.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 23/28] nfsd: allow encoding a filehandle into fattr4 without a svc_fh
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the encoding of a filehandle into fattr4 without requiring a svc_fh. It achieves this by adding a new 'fhandle' field to struct nfsd4_fattr_args and populating it from the svc_fh in CB_NOTIFY callbacks. This is necessary because the current fattr4 encoder requires a svc_fh, which is not available in all scenarios.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 24/28] nfsd: add a fi_connectable flag to struct nfs4_file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new flag, fi_connectable, to the nfs4_file structure in the Linux kernel's NFS server code. The flag indicates whether a filehandle should be encoded as connectable or not, based on whether subtree checking is enabled for the underlying directory delegation. This change addresses a specific requirement for handling CB_NOTIFY operations when no svc_export is available.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 25/28] nfsd: add the filehandle to returned attributes in CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new routine to compose a filehandle for use in CB_NOTIFY operations, which do not have access to the usual svc_export and require only a parent filehandle and nfs4_file. The new routine is used to fill out the fhandle field in the nfsd4_fattr_args structure. This allows the filehandle to be included in the attributes returned by CB_NOTIFY requests.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 26/28] nfsd: properly track requested child attributes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the way the NFS server tracks and encodes child attributes in delegation updates. It introduces a union of requested and supported child attributes, which are then encoded when sending add/remove/rename updates. This change ensures that only relevant attributes are sent, reducing unnecessary data transfer and improving performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 27/28] nfsd: track requested dir attributes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the handling of directory attributes in NFSv4 by tracking the union of requested and supported dir attributes in the delegation. It ensures that only the attributes present in this union are encoded when sending add, remove, or rename updates to the client. This change aims to prevent unnecessary attribute encoding and improve performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 28/28] nfsd: add support to CB_NOTIFY for dir attribute changes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support to CB_NOTIFY in the NFS server (nfsd) to send notifications about changes to directory attributes when requested by the client. The change is made to the nfs4state.c and nfs4xdr.c files, where a new condition is added to check if the client has requested dir attribute change notifications. If so, an additional event is sent alongside other events like add/remove/rename operations. This ensures that clients receive timely updates about directory attribute changes.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations, enabling the server to notify clients about changes within a directory without losing its lease. The patches modify various kernel components, including filelock, fsnotify, and nfsd, to implement this feature.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Identified a need to add support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations and proposed a solution by modifying various kernel components. The patches have been posted for review, but there are no substantive replies yet.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 0/4] mm: improve write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 0/4] mm: improve write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/4] mm: add NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY node page counter
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new per-node page counter called NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY to track the number of dirty pages that have been dirtied via RWF_DONTCACHE writes, also known as dropbehind writes. The counter is incremented when a folio is dirtied with the dropbehind flag set and decremented when the flag is cleared or the folio is cleaned. This information will be visible in /proc/vmstat and used by the writeback flusher to determine how many pages to write back during expedited writeback for IOCB_DONTCACHE writes, without flushing all dirty pages.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 2/4] mm: kick writeback flusher for IOCB_DONTCACHE with targeted dirty tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses performance issues in the IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback path by moving writeback submission off the writer's hot path and into a background flusher. The patch introduces a new WB_start_dontcache bit that uses targeted dirty tracking to limit the scope of expedited writeback, preserving I/O batching while reducing latency. The patch also adds a new writeback reason for tracing visibility and targets the correct cgroup writeback domain. Performance results show significant improvements in sequential write throughput and latency, as well as random write throughput and latency.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Andrew Morton questioned whether replacing inline filemap_flush_range() calls with a flusher kick would lead to a buildup of dirty, unreclaimable pagecache due to delayed writeback, and asked if there are mechanisms in place to limit this buildup.
review_comments.raw_body:
Naive questions...

On Sun, 26 Apr 2026 07:56:08 -0400 Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org> wrote:

> The IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback path in generic_write_sync() calls
> filemap_flush_range() on every write, submitting writeback inline in
> the writer's context.  Perf lock contention profiling shows the
> performance problem is not lock contention but the writeback submission
> work itself \u2014 walking the page tree and submitting I/O blocks the writer
> for milliseconds, inflating p99.9 latency from 23ms (buffered) to 93ms
> (dontcache).

So in the current case, when generic_write_sync() returns, all that
memory is written back and clean&reclaimable (or freed?), yes?

> Replace the inline filemap_flush_range() call with a flusher kick that
> drains dirty pages in the background.  This moves writeback submission
> completely off the writer's hot path.

Whereas after this change, that pagecache is probably still dirty,
unreclaimable, waiting for the flusher to do its thing?

So is there potential that the system will get all gummed up with
dirty, to-be-written-soon pagecache?  Is there something which limits
this buildup?

> ...
>
> dontcache-bench results on dual-socket Xeon Gold 6138 (80 CPUs, 256 GB
> RAM, Samsung MZ1LB1T9HALS 1.7 TB NVMe, local XFS, io_uring, file size
> ~503 GB, compared to a v6.19-ish baseline):
> 
>   Single-client sequential write (MB/s):
>                        baseline    patched     change
>   buffered              1449.8     1440.1      -0.7%
>   dontcache             1347.9     1461.5      +8.4%
>   direct                1450.0     1440.1      -0.7%
> 
>   Single-client sequential write latency (us):
>                        baseline    patched     change
>   dontcache p50         3031.0    10551.3    +248.1%
>   dontcache p99        74973.2    21626.9     -71.2%
>   dontcache p99.9      85459.0    23199.7     -72.9%
> 
>   Single-client random write (MB/s):
>                        baseline    patched     change
>   dontcache              284.2      295.4      +3.9%
> 
>   Single-client random write p99.9 latency (us):
>                        baseline    patched     change
>   dontcache             2277.4      872.4     -61.7%
> 
>   Multi-writer aggregate throughput (MB/s):
>                        baseline    patched     change
>   buffered              1619.5     1611.2      -0.5%
>   dontcache             1281.1     1629.4     +27.2%
>   direct                1545.4     1609.4      +4.1%
> 
>   Mixed-mode noisy neighbor (dontcache writer + buffered readers):
>                        baseline    patched     change
>   writer (MB/s)         1297.6     1471.1     +13.4%
>   readers avg (MB/s)     855.0      462.4     -45.9%

These results look ambiguous.  Sometimes better, sometimes worse?

> nfsd-io-bench results on same hardware (XFS on NVMe, NFSv3 via fio
> NFS engine with libnfs, 1024 NFSD threads, pool_mode=pernode,
> file size ~502 GB, compared to v6.19-ish baseline):
> 
>   Single-client sequential write (MB/s):
>                        baseline    patched     change
>   buffered              4844.2     4653.4      -3.9%
>   dontcache             3028.3     3723.1     +22.9%
>   direct                 957.6      987.8      +3.2%
> 
>   Single-client sequential write p99.9 latency (us):
>                        baseline    patched     change
>   dontcache            759169.0   175112.2     -76.9%
> 
>   Single-client random write (MB/s):
>                        baseline    patched     change
>   dontcache              590.0     1561.0    +164.6%
> 
>   Multi-writer aggregate throughput (MB/s):
>                        baseline    patched     change
>   buffered              9636.3     9422.9      -2.2%
>   dontcache             1894.9     9442.6    +398.3%
>   direct                 809.6      975.1     +20.4%
> 
>   Noisy neighbor (dontcache writer + random readers):
>                        baseline    patched     change
>   writer (MB/s)         1854.5     4063.6    +119.1%
>   readers avg (MB/s)     131.2      101.6     -22.5%

Ditto but less so.

> The NFS results show even larger improvements than the local benchmarks.
> Multi-writer dontcache throughput improves nearly 5x, matching buffered
> I/O. Dirty page footprint drops 85-95% in sequential workloads vs.
> buffered.

It sounds that you like the results, so OK ;)

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed Andrew Morton's concern that the patch could lead to a buildup of dirty, unreclaimable pagecache by deferring I/O submission to the flusher thread. The author explained that in this situation, writers are limited by backing device throughput and that peak pagecache with DONTCACHE writes was higher than with the unpatched version but still less than with normal buffered I/O. They also mentioned considering going back to submitting writes inline when the number of dirty pages is high, which could have a detrimental effect on performance.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Sun, 2026-04-26 at 05:28 -0700, Andrew Morton wrote:
> Naive questions...
> 
> On Sun, 26 Apr 2026 07:56:08 -0400 Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org> wrote:
> 
> > The IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback path in generic_write_sync() calls
> > filemap_flush_range() on every write, submitting writeback inline in
> > the writer's context.  Perf lock contention profiling shows the
> > performance problem is not lock contention but the writeback submission
> > work itself — walking the page tree and submitting I/O blocks the writer
> > for milliseconds, inflating p99.9 latency from 23ms (buffered) to 93ms
> > (dontcache).
> 
> So in the current case, when generic_write_sync() returns, all that
> memory is written back and clean&reclaimable (or freed?), yes?
> 

No. Before returning, it submits the I/Os for the portion that it wrote
rather than leaving it to the flusher to take care of things, but it
doesn't wait for the I/Os to complete.

> > Replace the inline filemap_flush_range() call with a flusher kick that
> > drains dirty pages in the background.  This moves writeback submission
> > completely off the writer's hot path.
> 
> Whereas after this change, that pagecache is probably still dirty,
> unreclaimable, waiting for the flusher to do its thing?
> 

Correct, but that's sort of the case today too since DONTCACHE I/Os
don't wait for the completion. With this change we're just deferring
the I/O submission to the flusher thread (which should hopefully soon
wake and take care of business). If the flusher thread can't keep up,
then eventually balance_dirty_pages() will kick in and start slowing
things down.

> So is there potential that the system will get all gummed up with
> dirty, to-be-written-soon pagecache?  Is there something which limits
> this buildup?
> 

Today in this situation, the writers are limited by the backing device
throughput. Once the I/O submission queues are full, then the DONTCACHE
writers end up stacking up on those. With this change, the writers will
be more limited by traditional VM limits in this situation. 

In the test runs I did, the peak pagecache with DONTCACHE writes was
higher than with the unpatched version but still considerably less than
with normal buffered I/O. That's the cost of deferring the I/O
submission to the flusher.

One thing we could consider is going back to submitting the writes
inline when the number of dirty pages is high. But, that could have a
detrimental effect on performance too.

game of tradeoffs. The good news is that DONTCACHE is still fairly new
and not many applications are using it yet, so the blast radius from
any change here should be rather small.

memory sizes. DONTCACHE gives us a place to experiment with this
scheme, but we may want to think about kicking off writeback earlier in
the normal buffered case too.

I suspect that it's because of the "scattered" nature of nfsd writes.
When the client sends a write to nfsd, we wake a nfsd thread to service
it. So, if there are a lot of writes operating in parallel, they all
get done in the context of different tasks.

My hunch is that this I/O pattern (writing to same file from a bunch of
different threads), particularly suffers from the DONTCACHE inline
write behavior. The threads all end up competing to submit jobs to the
queue and that causes the performance to fall off sharply.

>
> I suspect that it's because of the "scattered" nature of nfsd writes.
> When the client sends a write to nfsd, we wake a nfsd thread to service
> it. So, if there are a lot of writes operating in parallel, they all
> get done in the context of different tasks.
>
> My hunch is that this I/O pattern (writing to same file from a bunch of
> different threads), particularly suffers from the DONTCACHE inline
> write behavior. The threads all end up competing to submit jobs to the
> queue and that causes the performance to fall off sharply.
>

Thanks!

review_comments.author: Jan Kara
review_comments.summary: Jan Kara noted that the wb_check_start_dontcache() function does not handle the case where NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY is zero, and suggested adding a check to return -ENODATA in this scenario.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Sun 26-04-26 07:56:08, Jeff Layton wrote:
> The IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback path in generic_write_sync() calls
> filemap_flush_range() on every write, submitting writeback inline in
> the writer's context.  Perf lock contention profiling shows the
> performance problem is not lock contention but the writeback submission
> work itself \u2014 walking the page tree and submitting I/O blocks the writer
> for milliseconds, inflating p99.9 latency from 23ms (buffered) to 93ms
> (dontcache).
> 
> Replace the inline filemap_flush_range() call with a flusher kick that
> drains dirty pages in the background.  This moves writeback submission
> completely off the writer's hot path.
> 
> To avoid flushing unrelated buffered dirty data, add a dedicated
> WB_start_dontcache bit and wb_check_start_dontcache() handler that uses
> the new NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY counter to determine how many pages to write
> back.  The flusher writes back that many pages from the oldest dirty
> inodes (not restricted to dontcache-specific inodes). This helps
> preserve I/O batching while limiting the scope of expedited writeback.
> 
> Like WB_start_all, the WB_start_dontcache bit coalesces multiple
> DONTCACHE writes into a single flusher wakeup without per-write
> allocations.
> 
> Also add WB_REASON_DONTCACHE as a new writeback reason for tracing
> visibility, and target the correct cgroup writeback domain via
> unlocked_inode_to_wb_begin().
> 
> dontcache-bench results on dual-socket Xeon Gold 6138 (80 CPUs, 256 GB
> RAM, Samsung MZ1LB1T9HALS 1.7 TB NVMe, local XFS, io_uring, file size
> ~503 GB, compared to a v6.19-ish baseline):
> 
>   Single-client sequential write (MB/s):
>                        baseline    patched     change
>   buffered              1449.8     1440.1      -0.7%
>   dontcache             1347.9     1461.5      +8.4%

>

> Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-6
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

One comment regarding how the writeback is started:

> +static long wb_check_start_dontcache(struct bdi_writeback *wb)
> +{
> +	long nr_pages;
> +
> +	if (!test_bit(WB_start_dontcache, &wb->state))
> +		return 0;
> +
> +	nr_pages = global_node_page_state(NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY);
> +	if (nr_pages) {
> +		struct wb_writeback_work work = {
> +			.nr_pages	= wb_split_bdi_pages(wb, nr_pages),
> +			.sync_mode	= WB_SYNC_NONE,
> +			.range_cyclic	= 1,
> +			.reason		= WB_REASON_DONTCACHE,
> +		};
> +
> +		nr_pages = wb_writeback(wb, &work);
> +	}
> +
> +	clear_bit(WB_start_dontcache, &wb->state);
> +	return nr_pages;
> +}

So this will end up splitting NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY folios among per-cgroup wb
structures based on their writeback bandwidth. This is a reasonable thing
for global writeback where the bandwidth more or less corresponds to the
amount of dirty folios. However with DONTCACHE I expect big differences in
among NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY among different cgroups not necessarily
corresponding to wb throughput. In particular if you do DONTCACHE writes
from one cgroup and normal writes from another cgroup this will
systematically underestimate the amount needed to write by a factor of
about two.

So I think the stat should be a per bdi_writeback one (instead of a per node
one) which would avoid the need to split the value to wb here.

								Honza


-- 
Jan Kara <jack@suse.com>
SUSE Labs, CR

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 3/4] testing: add nfsd-io-bench NFS server benchmark suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a benchmark suite to test the performance of NFSD I/O modes using fio and the libnfs backend against an NFS server on localhost. The suite includes tests for buffered, direct, and cached I/O modes via NFSD debugfs controls. It also includes scripts to orchestrate the test matrix, parse results from fio JSON output, and configure the NFS export for testing. This patch aims to provide a comprehensive benchmarking tool for evaluating NFSD performance in various scenarios.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 4/4] testing: add dontcache-bench local filesystem benchmark suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new benchmark suite called 'dontcache-bench' to test the IOCB_DONTCACHE functionality on local filesystems using fio's io_uring engine and RWF_DONTCACHE flag. The suite includes various job files for different I/O patterns, such as sequential reads and writes, random reads and writes, and latency measurements. It also includes scripts to run and parse the benchmark results. This patch aims to provide a comprehensive testing framework for IOCB_DONTCACHE on local filesystems.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve write performance when using the RWF_DONTCACHE flag, which prevents caching of written data. The main change is a new scheme that kicks the flusher thread after a DONTCACHE write, causing it to perform writeback proportional to the amount written. This approach was suggested by Jan Kara and Christoph Hellwig during review of an earlier series. The patch also includes benchmark results in the last two patches, which show this method working as well or better than previous approaches.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed Andrew Morton's concern that the patch could lead to a buildup of dirty, unreclaimable pagecache by deferring I/O submission to the flusher thread. The author explained that this is not a new problem and that the writers are still limited by the backing device throughput. They also noted that in test runs, the peak pagecache with DONTCACHE writes was higher than with the unpatched version but still considerably less than with normal buffered I/O.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Sun, 2026-04-26 at 05:28 -0700, Andrew Morton wrote:
> Naive questions...
> 
> On Sun, 26 Apr 2026 07:56:08 -0400 Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org> wrote:
> 
> > The IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback path in generic_write_sync() calls
> > filemap_flush_range() on every write, submitting writeback inline in
> > the writer's context.  Perf lock contention profiling shows the
> > performance problem is not lock contention but the writeback submission
> > work itself — walking the page tree and submitting I/O blocks the writer
> > for milliseconds, inflating p99.9 latency from 23ms (buffered) to 93ms
> > (dontcache).
> 
> So in the current case, when generic_write_sync() returns, all that
> memory is written back and clean&reclaimable (or freed?), yes?
> 

No. Before returning, it submits the I/Os for the portion that it wrote
rather than leaving it to the flusher to take care of things, but it
doesn't wait for the I/Os to complete.

> > Replace the inline filemap_flush_range() call with a flusher kick that
> > drains dirty pages in the background.  This moves writeback submission
> > completely off the writer's hot path.
> 
> Whereas after this change, that pagecache is probably still dirty,
> unreclaimable, waiting for the flusher to do its thing?
> 

Correct, but that's sort of the case today too since DONTCACHE I/Os
don't wait for the completion. With this change we're just deferring
the I/O submission to the flusher thread (which should hopefully soon
wake and take care of business). If the flusher thread can't keep up,
then eventually balance_dirty_pages() will kick in and start slowing
things down.

> So is there potential that the system will get all gummed up with
> dirty, to-be-written-soon pagecache?  Is there something which limits
> this buildup?
> 

Today in this situation, the writers are limited by the backing device
throughput. Once the I/O submission queues are full, then the DONTCACHE
writers end up stacking up on those. With this change, the writers will
be more limited by traditional VM limits in this situation. 

In the test runs I did, the peak pagecache with DONTCACHE writes was
higher than with the unpatched version but still considerably less than
with normal buffered I/O. That's the cost of deferring the I/O
submission to the flusher.

One thing we could consider is going back to submitting the writes
inline when the number of dirty pages is high. But, that could have a
detrimental effect on performance too.

> > NFS engine with libnfs, 1024 NFSD threads, pool_mode=pernode,

game of tradeoffs. The good news is that DONTCACHE is still fairly new
and not many applications are using it yet, so the blast radius from
any change here should be rather small.

kick off writeback with normal buffered I/O, particularly with modern
memory sizes. DONTCACHE gives us a place to experiment with this
scheme, but we may want to think about kicking off writeback earlier in
the normal buffered case too.

> Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org> writes:

> > The IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback path in generic_write_sync() calls
> > filemap_flush_range() on every write, submitting writeback inline in

> > completely off the writer's hot path.

> > To avoid flushing unrelated buffered dirty data, add a dedicated
> > WB_start_dontcache bit and wb_check_start_dontcache() handler that uses
> > the new NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY counter to determine how many pages to write
> > back.  The flusher writes back that many pages from the oldest dirty
> > inodes (not restricted to dontcache-specific inodes). This helps
> > preserve I/O batching while limiting the scope of expedited writeback.
> >

> Yup, so, we wakeup the writeback flusher, which will write those many
> "number" of dirty pages. Those dirty pages written by writeback, can be
> of any type though, can be DONTCACHE or normal (non-dontcache) dirty
> pages. IIUC, writeback doesn't distinguish between them while writing.
> 

Correct. This was the approach that Jan and HCH suggested in the
responses to the last posting.

> 
> IMO, what we could also include in the commit msg is why is this above
> approach taken? IIUC, that is because, by writing NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY
> pages, it still reduces the page cache pressure and still reduces the
> amount of work that the reclaim has to do, even though some of those
> pages maybe non-dontcache pages, in case if there was a parallel
> buffered write in the system.
> 

Good suggestion. I'll add that.

> 
> Also should the following change be documented somewhere? Like in Man
> page maybe? i.e.
> Earlier RWF_DONTCACHE writes made sure that those dirty pages are
> immediately submitted for writeback and completion would release those
> pages. But now, in certain cases when there is a mixed buffered write in
> the system, those dontcache dirty pages might be written back after a
> delay (whenever the next time writeback kicks in).
> However for RWF_DONTCACHE reads, it should not affect anything.
> 

Looks like DONTCACHE is documented in the preadv/writev manpage. Here's
the current blurb about writes:

    Additionally, any range dirtied by a write operation with RWF_DONT‐
    CACHE  set  will  get kicked off for writeback.  This is similar to
    calling  sync_file_range(2)  with  SYNC_FILE_RANGE_WRITE  to  start
    writeback on the given range.  RWF_DONTCACHE is a hint, or best ef‐
    fort,  where  no hard guarantees are given on the state of the page
    cache once the operation completes.

I don't think this verbiage is invalid after this change. Kicking off
writeback is still just a hint, like it was before. We could mention
about how that I/O can compete with regular buffered I/O, but it seems
a bit like we're adding info that will just be confusing for users.

> > Like WB_start_all, the WB_start_dontcache bit coalesces multiple
> > DONTCACHE writes into a single flusher wakeup without per-write
> > allocations.
> > 
> > Also add WB_REASON_DONTCACHE as a new writeback reason for tracing

> > unlocked_inode_to_wb_begin().

> Since this patch series definitely affects that.
>
> BTW - adding these numbers in the commit msg itself is much helpful.
> 

To be clear, this only affects DONTCACHE, not normal buffered writes,
but I guess you're referring to the fact that DONTCACHE and buffered
writes can compete now.

Can you clarify specifically what you'd like me to test here? Are you
saying you want me to test parallel and buffered writes together at the
same time (i.e. make them compete?).

I should be able to do that for the local benchmarks, but nfsd's iomode
settings are global and that won't be possible there.

> Some explaination here of why 5x improvement with NFS compared to local
> filesystems please?
> (I am not much aware of NFS side, but a possible reasoning would help)

I suspect that it's because of the "scattered" nature of nfsd writes.
When the client sends a write to nfsd, we wake a nfsd thread to service
it. So, if there are a lot of writes operating in parallel, they all
get done in the context of different tasks.

My hunch is that this I/O pattern (writing to same file from a bunch of
different threads), particularly suffers from the DONTCACHE inline
write behavior. The threads all end up competing to submit jobs to the
queue and that causes the performance to fall off sharply.

Thanks for the review!

> normal buffered writes to file B.

Originally, I was benchmarking this via nfsd and only later did I add
the suite for local benchmarks. With nfsd, setting the iomode affects
all reads or writes.

So initially, I had it testing them with both reads and writes set to
the same setting, but then later I decided to play with different modes
for reads and writes. The best performing one was buffered reads +
dontcache writes. It's possible a mix of different modes will be better
on a local fs.

I can't easily do a benchmark like you're suggesting with nfsd, but it
should be possible on a local benchmark. I'll see what I can come up
with.

>   Mixed-mode noisy neighbor (dontcache writer + buffered readers):
>                        baseline    patched     change

review_comments.author: Ritesh (IBM)
review_comments.summary: Ritesh pointed out that the new writeback path for RWF_DONTCACHE writes may not distinguish between DONTCACHE and non-DONTCACHE dirty pages, potentially leading to delayed writeback of non-dontcache pages in mixed buffered write scenarios. He suggested documenting this change and testing parallel buffered writes and dontcache writes.
review_comments.raw_body:
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org> writes:

> The IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback path in generic_write_sync() calls
> filemap_flush_range() on every write, submitting writeback inline in
> the writer's context.  Perf lock contention profiling shows the
> performance problem is not lock contention but the writeback submission
> work itself — walking the page tree and submitting I/O blocks the writer
> for milliseconds, inflating p99.9 latency from 23ms (buffered) to 93ms
> (dontcache).
>
> Replace the inline filemap_flush_range() call with a flusher kick that
> drains dirty pages in the background.  This moves writeback submission
> completely off the writer's hot path.
>
> To avoid flushing unrelated buffered dirty data, add a dedicated
> WB_start_dontcache bit and wb_check_start_dontcache() handler that uses
> the new NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY counter to determine how many pages to write
> back.  The flusher writes back that many pages from the oldest dirty
> inodes (not restricted to dontcache-specific inodes). This helps
> preserve I/O batching while limiting the scope of expedited writeback.
>

Yup, so, we wakeup the writeback flusher, which will write those many
"number" of dirty pages. Those dirty pages written by writeback, can be
of any type though, can be DONTCACHE or normal (non-dontcache) dirty
pages. IIUC, writeback doesn't distinguish between them while writing.


IMO, what we could also include in the commit msg is why is this above
approach taken? IIUC, that is because, by writing NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY
pages, it still reduces the page cache pressure and still reduces the
amount of work that the reclaim has to do, even though some of those
pages maybe non-dontcache pages, in case if there was a parallel
buffered write in the system.


Also should the following change be documented somewhere? Like in Man
page maybe? i.e.
Earlier RWF_DONTCACHE writes made sure that those dirty pages are
immediately submitted for writeback and completion would release those
pages. But now, in certain cases when there is a mixed buffered write in
the system, those dontcache dirty pages might be written back after a
delay (whenever the next time writeback kicks in).
However for RWF_DONTCACHE reads, it should not affect anything.

> Like WB_start_all, the WB_start_dontcache bit coalesces multiple
> DONTCACHE writes into a single flusher wakeup without per-write
> allocations.
>
> Also add WB_REASON_DONTCACHE as a new writeback reason for tracing
> visibility, and target the correct cgroup writeback domain via
> unlocked_inode_to_wb_begin().
>
> dontcache-bench results on dual-socket Xeon Gold 6138 (80 CPUs, 256 GB
> RAM, Samsung MZ1LB1T9HALS 1.7 TB NVMe, local XFS, io_uring, file size
> ~503 GB, compared to a v6.19-ish baseline):
>

Can we please also test parallel buffered writes and dontcache writes? 
Since this patch series definitely affects that.

BTW - adding these numbers in the commit msg itself is much helpful.

>   Single-client sequential write (MB/s):
>                        baseline    patched     change
>   buffered              1449.8     1440.1      -0.7%
>   dontcache             1347.9     1461.5      +8.4%
>   direct                1450.0     1440.1      -0.7%
>

>   dontcache p99        74973.2    21626.9     -71.2%
>   dontcache p99.9      85459.0    23199.7     -72.9%
>
>   Single-client random write (MB/s):
>                        baseline    patched     change
>   dontcache              284.2      295.4      +3.9%
>
>   Single-client random write p99.9 latency (us):
>                        baseline    patched     change
>   dontcache             2277.4      872.4     -61.7%
>
>   Multi-writer aggregate throughput (MB/s):

Can you please help describe this test scenario if possible.. In above
you mentioned we are writing file_size as 2x RAM_SIZE. But your
multi-client tests says something else..

local num_clients=4
+	mem_kb=$(awk '/MemTotal/ {print $2}' /proc/meminfo)
+	client_size="$(( mem_kb / 1024 / num_clients ))M"

Also the multi-writer case is spawning parallel fio jobs, and then
parsing and aggregating the bandwidth results instead of using fio to
spawn multiple parallel threads... which is ok, but a bit wierd.
Why not let fio do the aggregate bandwidth, and latency calculation
instead?

>                        baseline    patched     change
>   buffered              1619.5     1611.2      -0.5%
>   dontcache             1281.1     1629.4     +27.2%
>   direct                1545.4     1609.4      +4.1%
>
>   Mixed-mode noisy neighbor (dontcache writer + buffered readers):
>                        baseline    patched     change
>   writer (MB/s)         1297.6     1471.1     +13.4%
>   readers avg (MB/s)     855.0      462.4     -45.9%
>
> nfsd-io-bench results on same hardware (XFS on NVMe, NFSv3 via fio
> NFS engine with libnfs, 1024 NFSD threads, pool_mode=pernode,
> file size ~502 GB, compared to v6.19-ish baseline):
>
>   Single-client sequential write (MB/s):
>                        baseline    patched     change
>   buffered              4844.2     4653.4      -3.9%
>   dontcache             3028.3     3723.1     +22.9%
>   direct                 957.6      987.8      +3.2%
>
>   Single-client sequential write p99.9 latency (us):

>

>   buffered              9636.3     9422.9      -2.2%
>   dontcache             1894.9     9442.6    +398.3%
>   direct                 809.6      975.1     +20.4%
>
>   Noisy neighbor (dontcache writer + random readers):
>                        baseline    patched     change
>   writer (MB/s)         1854.5     4063.6    +119.1%
>   readers avg (MB/s)     131.2      101.6     -22.5%
>
> The NFS results show even larger improvements than the local benchmarks.
> Multi-writer dontcache throughput improves nearly 5x, matching buffered
> I/O. Dirty page footprint drops 85-95% in sequential workloads vs.
> buffered.
>

Nice :)
Some explaination here of why 5x improvement with NFS compared to local
filesystems please?
(I am not much aware of NFS side, but a possible reasoning would help)

-ritesh

> Looks like DONTCACHE is documented in the preadv/writev manpage. Here's

>     Additionally, any range dirtied by a write operation with RWF_DONT\u2010
>     CACHE  set  will  get kicked off for writeback.  This is similar to
>     calling  sync_file_range(2)  with  SYNC_FILE_RANGE_WRITE  to  start
>     writeback on the given range.  RWF_DONTCACHE is a hint, or best ef\u2010
>     fort,  where  no hard guarantees are given on the state of the page
>     cache once the operation completes.
>
> I don't think this verbiage is invalid after this change. Kicking off
> writeback is still just a hint, like it was before. We could mention
> about how that I/O can compete with regular buffered I/O, but it seems
> a bit like we're adding info that will just be confusing for users.

Make sense.

>> > dontcache-bench results on dual-socket Xeon Gold 6138 (80 CPUs, 256 GB
>> > RAM, Samsung MZ1LB1T9HALS 1.7 TB NVMe, local XFS, io_uring, file size
>> > ~503 GB, compared to a v6.19-ish baseline):

>>

> but I guess you're referring to the fact that DONTCACHE and buffered
> writes can compete now.
>
> Can you clarify specifically what you'd like me to test here? Are you
> saying you want me to test parallel and buffered writes together at the
> same time (i.e. make them compete?).
>
> I should be able to do that for the local benchmarks, but nfsd's iomode
> settings are global and that won't be possible there.
>

The reason I am thinking of this is: dontcache marked pages, gets
evicted from page cache after they are written back. But this patch
series can now delay that from happening when there is a parallel
buffered writer dirtying page cache pages. Because of the reasons we
already discussed...

Note that, this may not be a workload which matters in the real world,
but I was thinking, it will be good to know the impact if any, of such
workload with this patch series (parallel buffered and dontcache
writers).

>> local num_clients=4
>> +	mem_kb=$(awk '/MemTotal/ {print $2}' /proc/meminfo)
>> +	client_size="$(( mem_kb / 1024 / num_clients ))M"
>> 

I guess you missed answering this. The reason why I was asking about this is....

>> >                        baseline    patched     change
>> >   buffered              1619.5     1611.2      -0.5%
>> >   dontcache             1281.1     1629.4     +27.2%
>> >   direct                1545.4     1609.4      +4.1%
>> > 

... If we see the performace of buffered and dontcache in baseline case,
then we don't see dontcache doing any good. Even the patched version is
just slightly better compared to buffered case.

But IIUC, dontcache should really shine in cases where we have buffered
writers dirtying the page cache pages which can overflow the RAM size
[1]. The reason why dontcache should show benefit there is, because we
don't see any page cache pressure, since after writeback the pages gets
evicted. Also earlier in the unpatched version, the I/O submission
happens immediately in the same context.

So, I guess, isn't it better to evaluate those scenarios as well with
the patched version - since this series affects those code paths now?

[1]: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20241110152906.1747545-11-axboe@kernel.dk/

>> 
>> Nice :)
>> Some explaination here of why 5x improvement with NFS compared to local
>> filesystems please?
>> (I am not much aware of NFS side, but a possible reasoning would help)
>> 
>
> I suspect that it's because of the "scattered" nature of nfsd writes.
> When the client sends a write to nfsd, we wake a nfsd thread to service
> it. So, if there are a lot of writes operating in parallel, they all
> get done in the context of different tasks.
>
> My hunch is that this I/O pattern (writing to same file from a bunch of
> different threads), particularly suffers from the DONTCACHE inline
> write behavior. The threads all end up competing to submit jobs to the
> queue and that causes the performance to fall off sharply.
>

Thanks!

-ritesh

> The NFS results show even larger improvements than the local benchmarks.
> Multi-writer dontcache throughput improves nearly 5x, matching buffered
> I/O. Dirty page footprint drops 85-95% in sequential workloads vs.
> buffered.
> 
> Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-6
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

One comment regarding how the writeback is started:

> +static long wb_check_start_dontcache(struct bdi_writeback *wb)
> +{
> +	long nr_pages;
> +
> +	if (!test_bit(WB_start_dontcache, &wb->state))
> +		return 0;
> +
> +	nr_pages = global_node_page_state(NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY);
> +	if (nr_pages) {
> +		struct wb_writeback_work work = {
> +			.nr_pages	= wb_split_bdi_pages(wb, nr_pages),
> +			.sync_mode	= WB_SYNC_NONE,
> +			.range_cyclic	= 1,
> +			.reason		= WB_REASON_DONTCACHE,
> +		};
> +
> +		nr_pages = wb_writeback(wb, &work);
> +	}
> +
> +	clear_bit(WB_start_dontcache, &wb->state);
> +	return nr_pages;
> +}

So this will end up splitting NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY folios among per-cgroup wb
structures based on their writeback bandwidth. This is a reasonable thing
for global writeback where the bandwidth more or less corresponds to the
amount of dirty folios. However with DONTCACHE I expect big differences in
among NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY among different cgroups not necessarily
corresponding to wb throughput. In particular if you do DONTCACHE writes
from one cgroup and normal writes from another cgroup this will
systematically underestimate the amount needed to write by a factor of
about two.

So I think the stat should be a per bdi_writeback one (instead of a per node
one) which would avoid the need to split the value to wb here.

								Honza


-- 
Jan Kara <jack@suse.com>
SUSE Labs, CR

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [RFC PATCH v2 1/2] vfs: syscalls: add mkdirat2() that returns an O_DIRECTORY fd
Re: [PATCH v3 2/4] mm: kick writeback flusher for IOCB_DONTCACHE with targeted dirty tracking
Re: [RFC PATCH v2 1/2] vfs: syscalls: add mkdirat2() that returns an O_DIRECTORY fd
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: A new syscall mkdirat2() is proposed to return an O_DIRECTORY fd, but it's unclear if this is necessary or how it would work.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the necessity and potential issues with implementing mkdirat2() that returns an O_DIRECTORY fd. Suggested a possible workaround using RPC calls to handle directory creation.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, 2026-04-28 at 07:39 +0100, Jeff Layton wrote:
> On Mon, 2026-04-27 at 17:48 +0200, Christian Brauner wrote:
> > 
> > 
> > And side-question: @Jeff, can nfs atomic open deal with O_CREAT |
> > O_DIRECTORY?
> > 
> 
> No, it can't. OPEN calls only work on regular files. This is why
> O_DIRECTORY works on NFS. If we end up issuing an OPEN against a
> directory, it'll fail, which is what we want in that situation.

To be clear, we could make that work by sending a second RPC:

    PUTFH+OPEN+....   (OPEN fails with NFS4ERR_ISDIR)

...and then send:

    PUTFH+CREATE...

...for a directory (which is how mkdir works in v4). If the calls race
with something else being created in its place, we could just open it
if it's a directory, or fail.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/2] x86/tdx: Port I/O emulation fixes

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 0/2] x86/tdx: Port I/O emulation fixes

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/2] x86/tdx: Fix off-by-one in port I/O handling
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes an off-by-one error in port I/O handling for TDX (Trusted Domain Extensions) on x86 architecture. The issue was caused by a mask calculation that produced a one-bit too wide result, leading to incorrect behavior for certain I/O sizes. The fix adjusts the mask calculation to correctly handle all I/O sizes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/2] x86/tdx: Fix zero-extension for 32-bit port I/O
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the x86/tdx code where 32-bit port I/O operations were not properly zero-extending their results to 64 bits. The fix updates the handle_in function to clear the entire RAX register when the I/O size is 4 bytes, ensuring that the result is correctly extended. This patch addresses a problem introduced by an earlier commit and has been reviewed by multiple developers.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses two technical inaccuracies in the TDX guest port I/O emulation code, specifically an off-by-one error and incorrect zero-extension for 32-bit port I/O operations. The first patch fixes the off-by-one error by adjusting the GENMASK() macro usage to match the correct bit width. The second patch ensures that 32-bit port I/O operations correctly zero-extend the result to the full 64-bit RAX register, as required by the x86 architecture. These issues were introduced in the initial implementation of the runtime hypercalls for port I/O and are fixed in this series.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: don't force DIO writes to be serialized

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: don't force DIO writes to be serialized

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses an issue in the Btrfs file system where Direct I/O (DIO) writes are being serialized due to a missing flag that was set before the transition to the new mount API in 2023. The flag, SB_NOSEC, tells the VFS that the filesystem may have files without security xattrs, allowing optimizations. Without this flag, DIO writes to the same file will be locked exclusively, leading to reduced performance. This patch removes the serialization by not forcing DIO writes to be exclusive locks, resulting in a significant improvement in DIO throughput, as demonstrated by fio benchmark results showing a 59% increase.

review_comments.author: David Sterba
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David Sterba requested more information about the fio script and the block group profile used for DIO writes, indicating a need for additional context before fully evaluating the patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
That's quite an improvement. What's the actual fio script you've used?
Also the DIO depends on the block group profile wrt the buffered
fallback so that would be good to know too.

review_comments.author: Mark Harmstone (author)
review_comments.summary: The author acknowledges a significant drop in DIO write performance in Linux 6.8, which was not recovered from, and plans to investigate automated performance testing to prevent similar issues.
review_comments.raw_body:
It is. There's a big dropoff in DIO write performance in 6.8 that we 
never recovered from. I'm going to look into some sort of automated 
performance so this kind of thing can't happen casually.

This was on a VM with 8 cores and 8GB of RAM, with a real NVMe exposed 
through PCI passthrough. The figures for XFS and ext4 in comparison are 
both about ~3GB/s.

# cat go
#!/bin/bash
mkfs.btrfs -f /dev/nvme0n1
mount /dev/nvme0n1 /mnt/test
mkdir /mnt/test/nocow
chattr +C /mnt/test/nocow
fio /root/test.fio

# cat /root/test.fio
[global]
rw=randwrite
ioengine=io_uring
iodepth=64
size=1g
direct=1
startdelay=20
force_async=4
ramp_time=5
runtime=60
group_reporting=1
numjobs=32
time_based
disk_util=0
clat_percentiles=0
disable_lat=1
disable_clat=1
disable_slat=1
filename=/mnt/test/nocow/fiofile
[test]
name=test
bs=4k
stonewall

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that there is an existing solution in iomap to handle bounded pages, which would eliminate the need for btrfs' specific fallback checks and potentially improve performance.
review_comments.raw_body:
There is the bounded page solution from iomap already, which will no 
longer fallback to buffered IO but to use extra page copy to make sure 
the final bio won't change its content halfway.

IIRC it's one extra flag and remove the btrfs' specific fallback checks, 
but I haven't yet verified the behavior/code.

Thanks,
Qu

review_comments.author: Mark Harmstone (author)
review_comments.summary: Author responded to Qu Wenruo's feedback by clarifying that the patch only addresses taking the inode rwsem exclusively, and is not related to serialization of DIO writes.
review_comments.raw_body:
That sounds like a different thing - this is just making it so that
we're not forced to take the inode rwsem exclusively for each write.

review_comments.author: David Sterba
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David Sterba requested that a test case be added to fstests/tests/perf, specifically for the 'pref' tests which are not yet caught up in the btrfs/fstests repository.
review_comments.raw_body:
As you've used fio for that, please add the test case to the
fstests/tests/perf. Though the 'pref' tests haven't caught up I'd like
to at least collect them in our btrfs/fstests repository.

review_comments.author: David Sterba
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch description does not mention any potential issues with the new mount API and its interaction with btrfs, specifically regarding IS_NOSEC flag and inode locking.
sentiment_signals: lack of discussion on potential issues
review_comments.raw_body:
I've updated changelog with the fio script and added the patch to
for-next. We want to get this backported to stable trees, ETA 2 weeks so
we get some coverage.

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH] mm: memcontrol: fix rcu unbalance in get_non_dying_memcg_end()
patches_acked.patch_summary: This patch fixes an RCU (Read-Copy Update) unbalance issue in the get_non_dying_memcg_end() function by removing the unnecessary setting of rcu_locked to false, which is not accessed in a specific build option. The fix allows for proper synchronization and prevents potential deadlocks or crashes. The patch has been acknowledged by Shakeel Butt.
